aliases: Butternut squash soup with seed butter toast

english: Butternut squash soup with seed butter toast

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for butternut squash soup; A00LC (vegetable-based soup) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for butternut squash soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of creamy butternut squash soup garnished with orange gremolata and sour cream, served alongside a slice of seed butter toast.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Butternut squash, cooked, baked, without salt (FDC ID 169295) and Soup, cream of vegetable, prepared with water (FDC ID 172457); composite estimate for restaurant-style preparation with cream and toast
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers this exact restaurant dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked butternut squash (FDC ID 169295), cream of vegetable soup (FDC ID 172457), sour cream (FDC ID 171257), and seed butter (sunflower seed butter, FDC ID 168596), weighted to reflect the described dish composition including the seed butter toast accompaniment. The input per_100g values (energy 137 kcal, protein 3.4 g, fat 8.6 g, carbohydrate 11.4 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 242.9 mg/100g is plausible for a restaurant soup with added salt and stock.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Pumpkin/squash soup, GI ~51 (analogous estimate); University of Sydney GI Database entry for butternut squash
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for this specific restaurant-style butternut squash soup with cream and toast. GI of 51 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for butternut squash (GI ~51, medium-low) and peer-reviewed data for cream of vegetable soup. The cream, protein, and fat content of this dish further moderate the glycaemic response relative to plain squash. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (51 × 11.43 g/100g × 350 g / 100) / 100 = (51 × 40.0 g) / 100 ≈ 20.4 / 100 × 100 = GL per 350 g serving: (51 × (11.43 × 3.5)) / 100 = (51 × 40.0) / 100 = 20.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 242.9 mg/100g; 350 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, approaching 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding table salt, and limit the seed butter toast accompaniment to one small slice of low-sodium wholegrain bread, as the combined sodium load of soup plus toast can approach half the recommended daily sodium ceiling in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 11.43 g/100g with GI ~51; 350 g serving delivers ~40 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 20, which is moderate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this soup in a standard 250–300 g bowl portion without the toast to keep the glycemic load below 15 per meal, and should monitor post-meal blood glucose given butternut squash's moderate glycemic index, noting that the fat and protein content of the soup attenuates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.43 g/100g and sodium 242.9 mg/100g; a 350 g serving contributes approximately 12 g saturated fat and 850 mg sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the soup prepared with reduced cream and sour cream to lower saturated fat intake, choose a seed butter made from sunflower or pumpkin seeds (not palm-based) for beneficial unsaturated fats, and limit the bread accompaniment to control total sodium and refined carbohydrate load per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High beta-carotene and vitamin C from butternut squash; saturated fat 3.43 g/100g is below the threshold for significant pro-inflammatory effect
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This soup is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory antioxidant profile of butternut squash; reducing cream and sour cream quantities will further lower saturated fat and minimise any pro-inflammatory dairy fat contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butternut Squash Soup is a creamy, blended Western-style soup made primarily from roasted or boiled butternut squash, enriched with cream and sour cream, and seasoned with vegetable stock, onion, garlic, salt, and pepper. As served in Singapore restaurants, it is garnished with an orange gremolata (orange zest, parsley, and olive oil) and a dollop of sour cream, and accompanied by a slice of seed butter toast made with sunflower or pumpkin seed butter. The soup has a smooth, velvety texture with a naturally sweet, nutty flavour from the squash, balanced by the acidity of the gremolata and the richness of the cream. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 137 kcal, 3.4 g protein, 8.6 g fat (3.4 g saturated), 11.4 g carbohydrate, 2.9 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 242.9 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 51 (low-medium), moderated by the fat and protein content. Butternut squash is a rich source of beta-carotene (provitamin A) and vitamin C, providing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (approximately 850 mg per 350 g serving) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, while the moderate glycemic load (~20 per serving) requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content from cream and sour cream is a consideration for cardiovascular health. This dish is popular as a starter or light meal in Western-style cafes and restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides exact nutrient data for this specific restaurant-style butternut squash soup with cream, sour cream, seed butter toast, and orange gremolata. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite entries for cooked butternut squash, cream of vegetable soup, sour cream, and seed butter, and are broadly consistent with the input per_100g values from the grabfood source. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis or exact HPB/USDA entry for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite restaurant-style butternut squash soup dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as a single matched entry as of 2024. The input ethnic_tags ('Cake', 'Pasta', 'Burger') appear erroneous for this dish and have been excluded from the output. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Foodo nutrient database and was not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for butternut squash and peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement for this exact dish preparation was found. Glycemic load per 350 g serving is approximately 20.4 (moderate). All core macros (protein_g 3.43, fat_g 8.57, carbohydrate_g 11.43) are non-zero and supported by the USDA composite estimate.
